text: HRV-guided daily training prescription produces greater improvements in maximal running velocity than predefined training programs over a 4-week period.
HRV-guided training can significantly improve both VO2peak and maximal running velocity, whereas predefined training may only improve maximal running velocity without significant VO2peak gains.

A practical HRV-guided training rule: prescribe high-intensity training when morning HRV increases or stays unchanged, and prescribe low-intensity or rest when HRV drops below a reference value (10-day mean minus 1 SD) or shows a decreasing trend for 2 consecutive days.
Morning high-frequency R-R interval oscillations (HF HRV) can serve as a daily biomarker to individualize training intensity prescription in endurance runners.

A 4-week HRV-guided training program using 40-minute running sessions at individually varied low or high intensity is sufficient to produce meaningful cardiorespiratory fitness improvements in moderately fit individuals.

A predefined program of 6 days/week training (4 high-intensity + 2 low-intensity sessions) improved maximal running velocity but not VO2peak over 4 weeks, suggesting the fixed high-intensity-heavy distribution may not optimally stimulate aerobic adaptation.
